label: 51_new start_arms control_arms_warheads
description: The New Strategic Arms Reduction Treaty, or New START, was signed in 2010 and required reductions in deployed strategic warheads. Analysts debated its importance for U.S. security and recommended strategies for future negotiations with Russia.

earliest_articles.title: Completing a New Arms Control Agreement with Russia
earliest_articles.excerpt: The United States and Russia are still at odds about a follow-on agreement to the Strategic Arms Reduction Treaty. This pact, a cornerstone of U.S.-Russian arms control negotiations, addresses reducing nuclear arsenals and combating proliferation of nuclear weapons. Brookings President Strobe Talbott expects the new…

earliest_articles.title: Resetting U.S.-Russian Leadership on Nuclear Arms Reductions and Nonproliferation
earliest_articles.excerpt: INTRODUCTION Vice President Biden announced the Obama administration’s intention to reset relations with Russia in a February 7, 2009 speech to the Munich Security Conference. Reset thereafter became the watchword as the administration set about restoring a U.S.-Russian relationship that, by the end of 2008, had…

newest_articles.title: The limits of nuclear arithmetic in a tripolar age
newest_articles.excerpt: The Trump administration’s decision to declare an end to the New START treaty seems to reaffirm a growing recognition among American strategists that legacy arms control frameworks—designed for a bipolar U.S.-Russia strategic order—are poorly suited to the new nuclear landscape, increasingly shaped by China’s rise.…

newest_articles.title: What comes after New START?
newest_articles.excerpt: The New Strategic Arms Reduction Treaty, known as the New START treaty, lapsed on February 5, 2026. U.S. President Donald Trump has said he will seek a better agreement, and Washington wants to bring in China and limit all Russian nuclear warheads, not just the deployed strategic warheads captured by New START. These…

newest_articles.title: How would Trump and Biden handle US nuclear policy upon reelection?
newest_articles.excerpt: Few issues are as potentially consequential as U.S. nuclear policy. What are Donald Trump and Joe Biden likely to do with respect to deterrence and arms control if reelected? Their previous terms in office suggest important differences—not only on specific nuclear policy issues but also in their overall approach to…

central_articles.title: New START and U.S. National Security
central_articles.excerpt: In testimony before the Senate Armed Services Committee, Steven Pifer explains why ratification of the new Strategic Arms Reduction Treaty is in the U.S. national interest. Pifer addresses concerns that have been raised about the treaty, noting how they either lack substantive basis or are over-stated relative to the…

central_articles.title: The future of U.S.-Russian arms control
central_articles.excerpt: Introduction The United States and Russia concluded the New Strategic Arms Reduction Treaty in 2010. New START entered into force in 2011, and by February 2018 each side is required to reduce its strategic forces to no more than 1,550 deployed warheads on no more than 700 deployed strategic missiles and bombers.
